title: any4 Learned 4-bit Numeric Representation for LLMs
abstract: We present any4, a learned 4-bit weight quantization solution for large language models (LLMs) providing arbitrary numeric representations without requiring pre-processing of weights or activations. any4 yields higher accuracy compared to other related 4-bit numeric representation types: int4, fp4 and nf4, as evaluated on a range of model sizes, generations and families (Llama 2, Llama 3, Mistral and Mixtral). While any4 does not require preprocessing of weights or activations, it is also competitive with orthogonal techniques that require such preprocessing (e.g., AWQ and GPTQ). We also experiment with any3 and any2 and show competitiveness at lower bits. Additionally, we show that we can calibrate using a single curated diverse sample rather than hundreds of samples from a dataset as done in most quantization approaches. We also open source tinygemm, a latency optimized GPU matrix multiplication library for LLMs, that implements any4 using a GPU-efficient lookup table strategy along with other common quantization methods. We open source our code at https://github.com/facebookresearch/any4.
impact: This paper presents a work that quantizes pretrained models. The input to the algorithm is a model’s pretrained weights, architecture, and a calibration dataset (which in our case was a single hand-written prompt). We have not evaluated if the quantization algorithm increases or decreases any societal impact of the underlying model. One factor that may introduce bias into the model is the calibration dataset. We leave it for future work to analyze the effect of different calibration datasets (or prompts in our case) on bias and truthfulness.

query_input.scenario: Edge deployment of LLMs on resource-constrained consumer devices
query_input.issue: Devices rely on pre-quantized LLMs from third parties, risking installation of malicious models.
hazard: Post-training quantization methods can be exploited to produce quantized models that behave maliciously while appearing benign in full-precision checks.
exposure: End users running on-device AI assistants on phones and smart gadgets.

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Resource-constrained devices lack integrity verification of quantized models, so users trust and install third-party AI assistants without security scrutiny.
hipporag_result.impact: Malicious on-device assistants exfiltrate personal data, produce harmful outputs, or enable remote device control.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Post-training quantization methods exploited to produce maliciously-behaving quantized models that appear benign in full-precision checks, through end users' on-device AI assistants on smart devices, trigger resource-constrained devices' lack of integrity verification, leading to data exfiltration, harmful outputs, or remote control.

dose_response: Exploitable quantization enables malicious quantized models to evade detection, which in edge deployment on consumer devices exposes end users, leading to persistent privacy breaches through on-device data exfiltration.
vulnerability: Consumer devices lack onboard integrity verification for quantized model weights.
Users cannot distinguish malicious model behavior from normal AI assistant errors.
Third-party app stores distribute quantized models without security review.
impact: Persistent privacy breaches through malicious on-device models exfiltrating personal data.
Physical harm when compromised AI assistants control smart home or health devices.
Mass exploitation via malicious model updates pushed through automatic app refresh cycles.
